explanations.definition: Reasons or information given to help someone understand something.
explanations.example: His explanations did not make the child feel better.

unhelpful.definition: Not useful or not making things better.
unhelpful.example: Saying 'you're tired' is often an unhelpful explanation.

situations.definition: The conditions or events happening at a particular time.
situations.example: Difficult situations arise when children are upset.

arise.example: Problems can arise when you least expect them.

certainty.definition: The feeling of being completely sure about something.
certainty.example: Parents often look for certainty about why a child is crying.

refusing.definition: Saying no or not being willing to do something.
refusing.example: The child was refusing to eat dinner.

curious.definition: Wanting to learn or know more about something.
curious.example: Stay curious about why your child is upset.

factor.definition: One of the things that affects a situation or result.
factor.example: Being tired was one factor in his bad mood.

fury.example: The wrong explanation added to her fury.

accurate.example: The description was not an accurate mirror of her feelings.

mirror.definition: Something that shows or reflects the truth of a situation.
mirror.example: His words were not a mirror of what she felt.

misunderstood.example: The child felt misunderstood when no one listened.

interpretations.definition: Ways of explaining or understanding the meaning of something.
interpretations.example: There are many interpretations for a child's behaviour.

harmful.example: Some explanations can be harmful for a child to hear.

recognizing.example: Recognizing your own patterns is the first step to change.

repair.definition: To fix something that is broken or damaged.
repair.example: You can repair the relationship by listening more.

secure.example: A child who feels secure doesn't need to act out.

maladaptive.definition: A way of coping that does not work well and causes more problems.
maladaptive.example: Screaming for attention is a maladaptive behaviour.

twigged.definition: Suddenly understood or realized something (informal British).
twigged.example: I twigged that she wanted attention, not an apple.

shamed.definition: Made to feel bad or embarrassed about something.
shamed.example: She was not shamed for wanting attention.

manipulative.definition: Trying to control people in a clever but unfair way.
manipulative.example: Toddlers are not being manipulative when they cry.

malice.definition: The wish to cause harm or hurt to someone on purpose.
malice.example: Young children do not act with malice.

aforethought.definition: Planned or thought about before doing something.

tantrum.definition: A sudden period of uncontrolled anger, usually by a child.
tantrum.example: The child had a tantrum in the shop.

kicking.example: The toddler was kicking during the tantrum.

strategy.definition: A plan made to reach a goal.
strategy.example: Screaming is not a pre-planned strategy for toddlers.

conveniently.definition: In a way that is easy and causes no trouble.
conveniently.example: Help your child express feelings more conveniently.

amateur.definition: Someone who is not professional or skilled at something.
amateur.example: The tantrum seemed like amateur dramatics.

dramatics.definition: Behaviour that is overly emotional, like acting in a play.
dramatics.example: Her crying seemed more like dramatics than real sadness.

trick.definition: To make someone believe something that is not true.
trick.example: The child was trying to trick her into skipping homework.

wind up.definition: To annoy or make someone angry (informal British).
wind up.example: She wasn't trying to wind me up, she just couldn't explain.

frustration.definition: The feeling of being annoyed because you cannot do what you want.
frustration.example: Children need help dealing with frustration.

impact.definition: A strong effect on something or someone.
impact.example: She had no idea of the impact her behaviour was having.

calculate.definition: To work out or figure out something carefully.
calculate.example: Young children cannot calculate how to upset you.

plonked.definition: Put down heavily or without care (informal British).
plonked.example: She plonked herself down on the floor and refused to move.

vocabulary.definition: All the words a person knows and uses.
vocabulary.example: Children learn vocabulary by listening to their parents.

model.definition: To show by example how to do something.
model.example: Parents model good behaviour for their children.

biscuit.definition: A small, flat, baked food, often sweet (British word for cookie).
biscuit.example: The child asked for a biscuit after lunch.

complicated.definition: Having many parts and hard to understand or do.
complicated.example: Learning to describe feelings is more complicated than asking for food.

social.definition: Related to being with other people and getting along with them.
social.example: Some children learn social skills more slowly.

flexible.definition: Able to change and adjust to new situations easily.
flexible.example: Young children take time to learn how to be flexible.

appropriate.definition: Right or suitable for a particular situation.
appropriate.example: Help your child find appropriate ways to express feelings.

fathom.definition: To understand something difficult or mysterious.
fathom.example: You will not always be able to fathom what is going on.

punitive.example: Being punitive does not help a child learn.

distress.definition: A feeling of great worry, sadness, or pain.
distress.example: Be kind to a child in distress.

nurture.definition: To care for and help someone grow and develop.
nurture.example: Kindness will nurture your relationship with your child.

hinder.definition: To make it harder for something to happen or develop.
hinder.example: Punishment can hinder your relationship with your child.

reassurance.definition: Words or actions that help someone feel less worried.
reassurance.example: Sometimes parents need reassurance about their child's behaviour.

peers.definition: People who are the same age or in the same group as you.
peers.example: Her behaviour seemed stuck compared to her peers.

therapist.definition: A trained person who helps people with emotional or mental problems.
therapist.example: A family therapist can help when things feel stuck.

diagnosis.definition: A doctor's decision about what illness or condition someone has.
diagnosis.example: The diagnosis helped them understand their child better.

relief.definition: A feeling of comfort when something difficult is over or understood.
relief.example: The diagnosis felt like a relief to the parents.

downside.definition: The bad part or disadvantage of something.
downside.example: The downside of a label is that it can close a door.

judgement.definition: A decision or opinion about someone or something.
judgement.example: A diagnosis can feel like a judgement on the child.

label.definition: A word or phrase used to describe someone, often unfairly.
label.example: A label can make people think things will never get better.

optimism.definition: The feeling that good things will happen in the future.
optimism.example: Don't lose your optimism about your child.

medicalized.definition: Treated as a medical problem when it may not need to be.
medicalized.example: Some normal behaviours become medicalized too quickly.

diagnosed.definition: Identified as having a particular illness or condition by a doctor.
diagnosed.example: More children born in August are diagnosed with ADHD.

disorder.definition: A medical condition where something in the body or mind does not work normally.
disorder.example: ADHD is a disorder that affects attention and activity.

mature.definition: Fully grown or developed in mind or behaviour.
mature.example: Younger children in a class may be less mature than older ones.

inhibit.definition: To stop or slow something from happening.
inhibit.example: Some medicine is designed to inhibit certain behaviours.

resort.definition: Something you do when everything else has failed.
resort.example: Medicine should be a very last resort.

cope.definition: To deal with something difficult successfully.
cope.example: If you cannot cope, seek professional help.

professional.definition: A person who is trained and skilled in a particular job.
professional.example: Seek professional help if you are struggling.

untangle.definition: To sort out something that is mixed up or confused.
untangle.example: It can take time to untangle bad habits.

habits.definition: Things you do regularly, often without thinking about it.
habits.example: Good habits help build strong relationships.
